title: Sola Gratia
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.point: I. Man's Part in Salvation: Death and Rebellion (vv. 1-3)
subpoints: A. All humanity is dead in trespasses and sins — not neutral, but rebelliously dead
B. Gentiles walked as sons of disobedience under the prince of the power of the air
C. Jews too were children of wrath by nature — the entire human race stands equally condemned
D. The dead cannot seek God — Romans 3:11: there is none who seeks after God

outline.point: II. But God — The Divine Initiative (vv. 4-5)
subpoints: A. 'But God who is rich in mercy because of His great love with which He loved us'
B. Even when we were dead in trespasses, God made us alive together with Christ
C. God is the one who raises — the main verb of the whole passage is 'made alive'
D. As Christ rose from the dead, so we become alive; as he ascended, we are seated in heavenly places

outline.point: III. Sola Gratia: Salvation Is by Grace Alone (vv. 8-9)
subpoints: A. 'By grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God'
B. Even the faith to believe is a gift of God — Paul anticipated the objection that faith is man's contribution
C. Grace by definition is unmerited favor — but more: we have actively demerit-ed, deserving wrath
D. Why does God save this way? — verse 7: to show the exceeding riches of his grace to the entire universe

outline.point: IV. Five Effects of Sovereign Grace in the Life of the Believer
subpoints: A. Obedience — we are God's workmanship (poem) created in Christ Jesus for good works prepared beforehand
B. Eternal life — the moment of faith secures life that never ends; we cannot be plucked from his hand
C. Heavenly blessings — we are already seated in heavenly places with Christ; we are in the kingdom now
D. Humility — sovereign grace crushes our pride; we are the object, not the subject, of our salvation
E. Dependence on God — convinced that each breath depends on his gracious act, we look to him for all things

summary:
The third in an October Reformation series that had already addressed Sola Scriptura and Solus Christus, this sermon takes up Sola Gratia — salvation by grace alone — from Ephesians 2:1-10. Pastor Lyro frames the sermon with the Reformation's core question: how can a sinner be reconciled to a holy God? For centuries, the church had taught a synergistic salvation in which human effort complemented Christ's work. The Reformers returned to Scripture and found something entirely different: a God who saves sinners completely, apart from any human contribution.

The sermon walks through Ephesians 2 to show what man's part in salvation actually is: death, rebellion, and enmity — a negative ten trillion. It is God who, in verses 4 and 5, breaks in with the decisive 'But God,' making dead sinners alive together with Christ. The basis for this salvation is stated in verses 8-9: by grace through faith, and even the faith itself is a gift. Paul closes the circle to prevent any boasting. The sermon ends by tracing five practical effects of sovereign grace in the life of the believer: obedience, eternal life, heavenly blessings, humility, and dependence on God. The vision of Ezekiel 37 — the valley of dry bones brought to life by the preached word — is used to dramatize the power of the word of God and the Spirit of God working together to raise dead sinners to life.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 2:1-10

This is the word of our Lord, Ephesians 2, starting at verse 1.

And you He made alive, who were dead in trespasses and sins, in which you once walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air, the spirit who now works in the sons of disobedience, among whom also we all once conducted ourselves in the lusts of our flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of the mind, and were by nature children of wrath, just as the others. But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you have been saved — and raised us up together and made us sit together in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus, that in the ages to come He might show the exceeding riches of His grace in His kindness toward us in Christ Jesus. For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast. For we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Lord, your word is powerful — it is more powerful than a two-edged sword — and we pray that this morning it would penetrate our hearts, it would lay our hearts open before you and before us, that we would know our hearts, that we would be conformed to Jesus Christ. We pray in his name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: Three Sundays in October

In these three Sundays in October we've been taking time to consider some of the doctrines that were important in the Reformation. We have considered the idea that the Word of God is the ultimate authority for us and for the church — the doctrine of Holy Scripture. We also considered the fact that Jesus Christ is the only Savior of God's elect — the doctrine of *Solus Christus*. I told some of you last week that today we would consider the doctrine that all these things — salvation and everything that happens — happen for the glory of God: *Soli Deo Gloria*. During the week, as I was thinking about this weekend and these important doctrines, I decided to shift that and we are going to consider another one — *Sola Gratia*: that salvation is by grace alone.

Now if you're thinking about the Reformation, which character comes to mind first? I think it's Martin Luther. And perhaps the words we associate with him are his defense at the Diet of Worms: "Unless I am convinced by the testimony of the Scriptures or by clear reason — for I do not trust either in the Pope or in councils alone, since it is well known that they often err and contradict themselves — I am bound by the Scriptures I have quoted, and my conscience is captive to the Word of God. I cannot and I will not retract anything. Here I stand — God help me."

But Luther was not alone. He had those who paved the way — men like John Wycliffe and John Huss — and those who built on his work: John Calvin, Ulrich Zwingli, Johannes Oecolampadius, John Knox, to whom we trace our own spiritual heritage.

These reformers were in strong agreement about five basic pillars of biblical theology — the doctrines that became known as the solas of the Reformation:

- *Sola Scriptura* — Scripture alone is our final authority for faith and practice.
- *Sola Gratia* — salvation is the product of the grace of God, and the grace of God alone.
- *Sola Fide* — that salvation comes to us through faith and faith alone.
- *Solus Christus* — that faith is in the only Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ.
- *Soli Deo Gloria* — all of this happens to the glory and praise and honor of God.

These five pillars provide the framework for other equally important doctrines rediscovered by the Reformers. They were formulated in the context of a particular question: the doctrine of justification by grace alone through faith alone — how can a sinner be reconciled to a holy God? That was the core of the 16th century Reformation.

For about a thousand years the church had been teaching that you and I could help God in saving our own souls. There was a part that was God's part, but there was a part that was ours — we had to add to the obedience of Christ by our own obedience in order to have enough to reach heaven. This is the teaching that our salvation was *synergistic* — that we and God worked together as partners. The problem is that the Bible doesn't teach that.

## I. Man's Part in Salvation (vv. 1-3)

I want you to notice what Paul tells us about man's part in salvation. Do you see it as you read those first three verses?

We bring death and sin. That's what we're bringing to the table.

Notice the negative tone when Paul speaks of mankind. All humanity is dead in verse 1. Not just neutrally dead — rebelliously dead. Dead and still in enmity and anger toward God. And not just some: all. In verse 2, the Ephesians — Gentiles — are called "sons of disobedience" to demonstrate their rebellion. Their whole life is a life of disobedience. That's what you and I are without Christ. If you are without Christ, you are walking according to the prince of darkness. Your lord is Satan himself. There are only two options: alive in Christ, or dead in Satan. There is no third option.

And not only the Gentiles — in verse 3, Paul says "we Jews" are also in the same boat apart from Christ. Jews apart from Christ are equally sons of the devil.

Notice that Paul declares all people not only dead in rebellion, but serving the devil. And because the unbeliever is dead and in rebellion, he or she cannot do anything to turn to God.

A homiletics professor at seminary would require his students, as part of their first year, to go to the cemetery and preach to the graves. Students complained: isn't this a waste of time? The professor said no — because that is exactly what you are going to be doing Lord's Day after Lord's Day if there are unbelievers in your midst. You're preaching to dead people, hoping they rise from the grave. That's exactly what happens when an unbeliever responds to the gospel — they were not just sick; they were dead, and now they are made alive.

Romans 3:11: "There is none who seeks after God." Since this is the case, there is no good work that can be done, nothing good that we can bring. The unbeliever does not want to please God. Romans 3:12: "They have all turned aside; they have together become unprofitable; there is none who does good, no, not one."

Brothers and sisters, even though the world's unbelievers are dead in their trespasses and sins, it is the message of the gospel — the foolishness of a man dying for others — that raises people from their death. I love Ezekiel 37, the passage where God takes Ezekiel to a valley of very dry bones. Ezekiel says, "Lord, you know." And the Lord says, "Preach my word. Just do it." Ezekiel proclaims and the bones begin to rattle — the thigh bone connects to the knee bone, the knee bone to the shin bone — and Ezekiel is doing nothing but proclaiming the word of God. And then the Spirit of life comes into those bones. That is exactly what Paul is talking about here in Ephesians 2 — it is the word of God by the grace of God in the hands of the Spirit of God that works in the hearts of dead people to bring them to life.

## II. But God — The Divine Initiative (vv. 4-5)

So what is our part in salvation? Nothing. We are running away from God and God reaches in, takes that heart of stone, gives us a heart of flesh that is able to believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. By the time we come to faith in Christ, we come most willingly — no one comes kicking and screaming to Christ. But that's the case because God has already regenerated us. He's already given us that new life, that heart that now wants to embrace the Lord Jesus Christ.

And after the description of mankind without Christ, we come to verse 4 and find some glorious words: "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you have been saved."

That's God's part in salvation. Man's part? Zero. As a matter of fact — negative ten trillion. God's part: all of it.

Despite our being dead and rebellious, God chose a people for himself and loved them in Christ. In chapter 1:11-12, we learn that he did that for the praise of his glory. Realize that in eternity past, God decided that saving you would glorify him more than anything else. You look at yourself and say, "Me?" Yes — because it is his work, not yours. It is who he is, not who you are, that brings glory to himself.

He showed his love by making us alive with Christ in verse 5. This is the main verb of the whole passage — God is the one who makes dead, rebellious sinners alive. And as Christ in verse 6 rose from the dead, so we become alive. As Christ ascended to heaven, so we are brought up to heavenly places with Christ.

## III. Sola Gratia: Salvation by Grace Alone (vv. 8-9)

What is the basis of all this? Verses 8-9: "For by grace you have been saved through faith, and that not of yourselves; it is the gift of God, not of works, lest anyone should boast."

Grace by definition is unmerited favor in the place of merited wrath. However, it is not sufficient to define grace just as unmerited favor. As sinners, we have not just failed to do enough — we have actively demerit-ed. We've done enough to not deserve it at all. We deserve hell. And grace is God's blessing freely bestowed on ill-deserving sinners.

Furthermore, even the faith to trust in Christ for salvation is a gift of God. Paul anticipated an argument that would be made centuries after Ephesians was written: that yes, salvation is of God, but the faith is mine — faith is what I bring to the table, what makes me worthy of being saved. Paul says: both grace and faith are gifts of God. He knew that man would try to boast about his faith. So he says — nope, faith is also a gracious gift.

Why did God do this this way? Verse 7 tells us: "that in the ages to come He might show the exceeding riches of His grace and His kindness toward us in Christ Jesus." God chose to have justification be by grace alone through faith alone to show the universe his grace. He designed it so that the entire creation would know he is a gracious God. Every time a sinner is saved, God's grace and kindness are displayed.

He also tells us in chapter 1:11 that doing this this way brings the most possible glory to himself. Saving you through the death, resurrection, and ascension of his Son brings the most glory to himself. Every time someone tries to make salvation work another way, they are robbing God of the glory due to him.

## IV. Five Effects of Sovereign Grace

**First: Obedience.** Verse 10: "we are His workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works which God prepared beforehand that we should walk in them." The word "workmanship" here is the translation of a Greek word from which we get the English word "poem." We are God's poem — the result of God's creative mind in Jesus Christ. And this word for "created" here is only used in the New Testament with God as the subject. It's the kind of creation only God can do. Obedience flows from sovereign grace working in our hearts.

**Second: Eternal life.** Verse 7 says this salvation will be displayed for the ages to come. We are eternally made alive in Christ, never to die again. "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life" — the moment of faith is so certain that you have eternal life right now. Jesus has you in his hands, and the Father has his hands around Jesus's hands. Nobody can unclasp them. God has written your name in the palm of his hand.

**Third: Heavenly blessings.** Verse 6: we are currently in heavenly places with Christ. We are in the kingdom of God *now*. We've been translated out of the kingdom of darkness into the kingdom of the Son he loves. We are adopted sons and daughters who enjoy the benefits of that adoption now. The first question of our catechisms asks what our chief end is — to glorify God and enjoy him forever — and we tend to focus on "forever" as something future. But the forever includes *now*. We get to enjoy him now.

**Fourth: Humility.** Sovereign grace crushes our pride. We want to be the subject, not the object, of our salvation. We want to be active, not passive, in the process. By nature we rebel against sovereign grace. But God knows how to break our rebellion and make us friends of this doctrine. If you are fighting against the sovereign grace of God right now, submit to it — you are going to lose that battle. And when God teaches sinners that their very core is depraved, sovereign grace becomes the most encouraging doctrine imaginable, because it means that your salvation depends entirely on an unchangeable God, not on you.

**Fifth: Dependence on God.** If we are convinced that the next breath we take depends on the gracious act of God, we will look to him for all things. And when we look to him for all things, he will be even more gracious to us.

## Closing

The doctrine of justification by grace alone is a wonderful doctrine. To know that our salvation depends only on the decree of an unchangeable God should bring great comfort to our hearts. This free sovereign grace cries out for expression in the church today. The church as a whole has lost its grasp on the sovereignty of God. But only the gospel of sovereign grace will capture and transform sinners by the power of the Word and the Spirit of God.

Are we sons and daughters of the Reformation? Are we willing to say — and to sing — "O, to grace how great a debtor, daily I'm constrained to be; let that grace now, like a fetter, bind my wandering heart to thee"? Do you believe that? To know that is to be secure in the hands of the Lord.

Let's pray together. Lord our God, we thank you that you are a God who saves, and that you sovereignly save by your grace. We are overjoyed. In Jesus' name, amen.
